title: Muscovy ducks for meat production
subtitle: A practical guide to raising Muscovies from ducklings to table weight.
excerpt: Muscovy ducks are the quietest, most self-sufficient table duck available to the UK smallholder. They forage actively, require minimal purchased feed in summer, and produce a lean, dark-fleshed bird quite different from commercial ducks. A drake and four ducks, left to their own devices on a decent paddock, will produce two or three clutches a season.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Ducks (2010), gov.uk. Lewis Wright, The Practical Poultry Keeper (1867), Internet Archive: duck management chapter. AHDB poultry species summary, ahdb.org.uk.

glossaryTerms.definition: Cairina moschata, a species of duck originating in Central and South America, not directly related to the mallard-derived breeds (Pekin, Khaki Campbell, etc.). Muscovies are largely silent, strong fliers if not pinioned, and produce a lean, gamey meat compared to commercial ducks. The drake is significantly larger than the duck.

The male Muscovy duck. Distinguished by the fleshy red caruncles around the beak and eyes, and by his size: a mature drake weighs 4 to 6 kg, more than twice the duck's weight. He is slower to mature than the duck and will not be ready for the table at the same age.

A young Muscovy duck from hatch to approximately 8 weeks of age. Muscovy ducklings are slower to feather than mallard-derived breeds, and they are susceptible to cold and wet in their first two weeks.

content.text: is the only domestic duck that is not derived from the mallard. The difference shows in the meat: darker, denser, and with a lower fat content than a Pekin or Aylesbury. For the smallholder who wants a duck that forages hard and needs minimal management, Muscovies are worth considering seriously.

Muscovies are strong fliers and will roost in trees if not given a suitable elevated perch inside a house. Wing clipping at one wing, or pinioning ducklings in the first days of life, is the common management approach for those who want to keep them contained. A secure wooden house with a ramp for access and straw bedding is adequate. They do not require water deep enough to swim in for welfare, but access to water at drinker height at minimum is required.

The minimum space guidance for ducks applies: at least 1 square metre of indoor space per bird. Muscovies use outdoor space intensively when available. A run area with vegetation, areas of disturbed ground to probe, and some shade suits them well.

is substantially larger than the females and matures more slowly: drakes reared for the table take 16 to 20 weeks compared to 12 to 14 weeks for ducks. The size difference matters for slaughter day planning. A ratio of one drake to four or five ducks is standard for breeding groups. Too many drakes causes stress to the females.

Muscovy ducks are determined brooders. A duck will lay a clutch of 8 to 16 eggs in a sheltered nest site and sit them for 35 days, the longest incubation period of any domestic poultry. She broods her own ducklings reliably, so artificial incubation is not necessary if the breeding group is managed for natural rearing. The main risk is nest exposure to predators; provide a nest box placed low in the house with a wooden front the duck can step over but a fox cannot easily access.

under a heat lamp at 32 C for the first week, reducing by 3 C each week until they are on ambient temperature. They are slower to feather than chickens and should not be moved outdoors in cold or wet weather until fully feathered, usually around 8 weeks. Feed chick crumb for the first four weeks, then move to pellets or grain.

Muscovies on good pasture in summer will forage slugs, insects, and vegetation significantly. Supplement with wheat and a waterfowl grower pellet. Avoid medicated chick starter: Muscovies are sensitive to some coccidiostats used in chicken feeds. Check the label before feeding chick crumb from a general poultry range.

Female Muscovies reach 2 to 3 kg liveweight at 12 to 14 weeks; drakes reach 4 to 5 kg at 16 to 20 weeks. Slaughter before the first moult (around 10 weeks) for ducks, or after the moult has been completed (after 20 weeks) for drakes; birds in active moult are difficult to pluck cleanly due to pin feathers. For home slaughter of ducks, the same poultry slaughter regulations apply as for chickens: stunning before slaughter or the neck dislocation method for small numbers.

Muscovies are the quietest domestic waterfowl by a large margin. Ducks produce a soft, breathy hiss; drakes make almost no sound. This makes them suitable for smallholdings with close neighbours where the constant quacking of Khaki Campbells or Welsh Harlequins would cause friction. They are not silent when disturbed, but the volume is a fraction of most other duck breeds.
